coredata.dc:title: A new green technology for direct synthesis of carbon nanodots with narrow size distribution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Lee, Seungae
coredata.dc:description: Highly monodisperse carbon nanodots are synthesized by a surfactant-free polymerization and subsequent carbonization process, which is a straightforward and environmental-friendly approach. Uniform polypyrrole nanoparticles with a diameter of ca. 10 nm are successfully fabricated using low temperature polymerization. Subsequent carbonization of polypyrrole leads to the formation of carbon nanodots having an average diameter of 8 nm. Structural information analysis is carried out to clarify the structure of carbon nanodots. Moreover, spectroscopic investigation on the carbon nanodots is conducted to achieve an in-depth examination on the characteristics of carbon nanodots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Green technologies
Environmental-friendliness
Polypyrrole
Uniformity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations for the synthesis of carbon nanodots. The polymerization of pyrrole is carried out at low temperature (3 °C) using ammonium persulfate as an initiator and extremely small amount of poly(vinyl alcohol) as a stabilizer. Subsequent carbonization at 900 °C and filtration yield monodisperse carbon nanodots.

TEM images of (a) polypyrrole nanoparticles having a diameter of ca. 10 nm taken at low magnification (inset: high-magnification TEM image of a polypyrrole nanoparticle), and (b) at high magnification, (c) carbon nanodots with a diameter of ca. 8 nm taken at low magnification (inset: high-magnification TEM image of a carbon nanodot), and (d) at high magnification.
XPS spectra of carbon nanodots. (a) Survey spectrum. (b) C1s spectrum. (c) O1s spectrum. (d) N1s spectrum.

(a) Raman spectrum of carbon nanodots. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of polypyrrole nanoparticles and carbon nanodots. (c) PL emission spectra of carbon nanodots and blue phosphor BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+ (BAM). (d) PL emission spectra of carbon nanodots at different excitation wavelengths.
Elemental analysis of polypyrrole nanoparticles and carbon nanoparticles.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The values for C, H, N, and S were obtained by elemental analyzer (EA1110, CE Instrument).

head.ce:title: A new green technology for direct synthesis of carbon nanodots with narrow size distribution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konkuk University, 120 Neungdong-ro, Gwangjin-gu, Seoul 05029, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly monodisperse carbon nanodots are synthesized by a surfactant-free polymerization and subsequent carbonization process, which is a straightforward and environmental-friendly approach. Uniform polypyrrole nanoparticles with a diameter of ca. 10 nm are successfully fabricated using low temperature polymerization. Subsequent carbonization of polypyrrole leads to the formation of carbon nanodots having an average diameter of 8 nm. Structural information analysis is carried out to clarify the structure of carbon nanodots. Moreover, spectroscopic investigation on the carbon nanodots is conducted to achieve an in-depth examination on the characteristics of carbon nanodots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials, including diamond, carbon nanotube (CNT), graphite and graphene, have been widely studied for many years because of their fascinating properties such as thermal stability, mechanical properties and cost effectiveness [1–3]. Especially, carbon nanodots which possess at least one dimension less than 10 nm and photoluminescence (PL) have received a great deal of attention due to their numerous characteristics such as outstanding photochemical stability, tunable excitation-emission properties, low cost, non-toxicity and good biocompatibility [4,5]. After discovering carbon nanodots in 2004, a large effort has been devoted to develop effective synthetic strategies to prepare carbon nanodots [6]. Generally, the carbon nanodots are obtained through two distinct approaches, i.e. top-down and bottom-up approaches [7,8]. Top-down approach is to prepare the carbon nanodots by cutting different carbon sources, such as graphite, carbon fibers, carbon black, etc. Distinguished from the top-down method, bottom-up routes are to synthesize the carbon nanodots from organic molecules or polymers. Although comprehensive approaches have been proposed for synthesizing carbon nanodots, most formation processes require complicated steps and result in high polydispersity and low yield, which indicates that it is still challenging to design a synthetic route for controlling the size and shape of carbon nanodots through easy and straightforward steps.

It has been reported that the amorphous carbon dots are relatively easy to synthesize as well as inexpensive [9]. In addition, the amorphous carbon nanodots have fascinating optical, chemical and electronic properties as great as the crystalline carbon dots [10,11]. Moreover, the interesting thing is that the spectroscopic properties of the carbon dots are similar with that of graphene quantum dots [12]. However, most strategies for preparing the amorphous carbon dots involves high temperatures and purification processes [13–17]. Furthermore, in some cases, surface stabilization is required by adding inorganic additives or passivating agents [18,19]. Most preferred synthetic approaches of amorphous carbon dots are hydrothermal or thermal methods using carbon precursors such as polymers and carbohydrates [20,21]. However, there are still limitations that it is not easy to control the size of carbon dots and obtain the uniform shape by simple process, as well as that the synthetic process should be followed by surface passivation.

Here, we describe a green technology to produce monodisperse carbon nanodots via carbonization of polypyrrole nanoparticles (PPy NPs) which is a simple and reliable method. This approach is a breakthrough to synthesize carbon nanodots with controlled size and shape. Importantly, it is worth nothing that the PPy NPs having a diameter of ca. 10 nm are prepared without any surfactants, which is an innovative green technology for environment. The prepared carbon nanodots have highly uniform shape and size with an average diameter of 8 nm and show attractive spectroscopic characteristics relevant to amorphous carbon nanodots.

Polypyrrole nanoparticles were synthesized by a green synthesis approach which is a modified dispersion polymerization method. A small amount of poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, Mw: 9000 g mol–1) was dissolved in distilled (DI) water by magnetic stirring at room temperature. The concentration of PVA solution used for the synthesis of PPy is only about 0.002 wt%, which is extremely small compared to the previous dispersion polymerization method [22–24]. Pyrrole (98%) monomer were added to the PVA solution, followed by magnetic stirring at 3 °C. With a continuous stirring at 3 °C, ammonium persulfate (APS) was added to initiate the polymerization. The polymerization proceeded for 3 min at 3 °C under continuous stirring at a stirring speed of 600 rpm, followed by washing process with ethanol to remove the residual PVA and other reagents. The resulting product was dried in a vacuum oven at room temperature.

To obtain the carbon nanodots, the polypyrrole nanoparticles were utilized as carbon precursors. The carbonization process was carried out using a quartz tubular furnace (21100 Tube furnace, Barnstead Thermolyne Corporation, USA) under Ar flow. The samples were heated up to 900 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C min–1, and held for 3 h at 900 °C, and cooled to room temperature. The char yield was approximately 63%, estimated by measuring the weight of samples before and after the carbonization process.

To completely remove the residual PVA and to get an enhanced intensity of PL spectra, the vacuum filtration was carried out using anodic aluminum oxide (AAO, Anodisc, 100 nm pore size, Whatman) membrane. Uniform carbon nanodots without residual carbonized PVA were obtained by vacuum filtration of the as-prepared materials dispersed in DI water through a porous AAO filter. Through the subsequent washing and drying processes, the uniform carbon nanodots were acquired.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using a JEM-2100 (JEOL). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) signals were investigated using XPS (Sigma Probe, Thermo-VG, UK) with a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.7 eV) X-ray source. For the structure analysis of carbon nanodots, Raman spectrum was recorded using a Raman spectrometer T64000 (HORIBA) with a 514 nm Ar laser. Elemental analysis (C, H, N, and S) was carried out using a CHNS-932 (LECO). UV spectra were acquired by Lambda 35 double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by LS 45 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA).

Highly uniform carbon nanodots are acquired by using the PPy NPs as carbon precursors. The PPy NPs with an average diameter of ca. 10 nm are synthesized by a modified dispersion polymerization which involves low-temperature polymerization. An extremely small amount of PVA is used as a stabilizer to inhibit particle aggregations (Fig. 1 ). The low temperature polymerization facilitates the formation of smaller particle with narrow size distribution by slowing down the polymerization rate, namely, slowing down the initiation of APS. Higher temperatures induce more formation of radicals from initiators, which means there is a larger concentration of radicals in reacting solution, leading to initially faster polymerization than at lower temperatures. Faster initial polymerization leads to faster increase in viscosity, indicating it is more difficult for unreacted monomer to find growing radicals, and for unreacted initiator to find monomer because the increase in viscosity slows the ability of molecules to diffuse in solution. Consequently, higher temperature results in non-uniform particle size distributions [25]. Through a carbonization process at 900 °C, the PPy NPs are transformed into carbon nanodots with an average diameter of ca. 8 nm. To completely remove the carbonized PVA residue, the filtration process is carried out using anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) membrane. Through the additional filtration, not only the carbonized PVA residue is completely removed but also the intensity of the PL spectra for the carbon nanodots is significantly enhanced because of the size uniformity of carbon nanodots.

The PPy NPs are fairly monodisperse, judging from the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 2 a and b). An average diameter of the PPy NPs is ca. 10 nm, and it can be controlled by adjusting the concentration of monomer [26,27]. Through the carbonization process at 900 °C, the PPy NPs are graphitized with diminishing their diameter to ca. 8 nm (Fig. 2c and d). Particle size reduction is ascribed to the graphitization which involves heat energy transfer from the surface to the center of PPy NPs. The dehydrogenation and aromatization during the carbonization process results in the compact structure. The loss of nitrogen atoms occurs between 400 and 600 °C [28,29]. Through the dehydrogenation and aromatization of the PPy NPs, the carbon nanodots are acquired. To obtain the carbon nanodots with less oxygen functional groups, the carbonization process was carried out at 900 °C. It has been reported that more oxygen functional groups are removed as the carbonization temperature increases [30]. The uniformity in shape and size of nanoparticles is maintained even after the carbonization process. Furthermore, the size of carbon particles can be controlled with adjusting the diameter of PPy NPs. For instance, the carbonization of PPy NPs with an average diameter of 30, 15, and 10 nm yields the carbon nanoparticles with an average size of 25, 10, and 8 nm respectively (Fig. S1).

To examine the structural information of the carbon nanodots, X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) signals are measured (Fig. 3 a). The survey spectrum of the carbon nanodots shows three typical peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, which indicates that the carbon nanodots are mainly composed of C (89.94%). In addition, the peaks at 977 and 497 eV correspond to O KLL and Na KLL peak respectively. Moreover, the peaks at 299, 162 and 63 eV correspond to S 2s, S 2p and Na 2s respectively [31]. The spectrum of C1s is deconvoluted into several single peaks that correspond to C–C/CC (284.5 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), C–O (286.8 eV), CO (288 eV) and OC–O (289.4 eV) (Fig. 3b). The O1s spectrum confirms the existence of CO (531.4 eV), C–O (532.9 eV) and C–O–C (533.9 eV), and the content of oxygen is much smaller than carbon (Fig. 3c). The spectrum of N1s reveals three nitrogen species of pyridinic N (398.3 eV), pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) and graphitic N (400.7 eV) (Fig. 3d). As shown in the survey spectrum, the carbon nanodot contains extremely small amount of nitrogen (1.42%) [32–35].

The elemental constituents of the PPy NPs and carbon nanodots were investigated by elemental analysis (Table 1 ). The C/N ratio of the PPy NPs is ca. 4.0, since there are a nitrogen atom and four carbon atoms in a repeating unit of PPy. The C/N ratio significantly increases up to ca. 46, which verifies that the PPy NPs were carbonized to form the carbon nanodots. The atomic percentage in carbon nanodots acquired by the elemental analysis is similar to the results from the XPS analysis.

For the structural characterization, Raman spectrum of the carbon nanodots is measured (Fig. 4 a). Two typical peaks of carbon materials are observed in the Raman spectrum of carbon nanodots. The peat at 1592 cm–1 corresponds to the in-plane vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms denoting the E2g mode of graphite (G band). The band at 1350 cm–1 is assigned to the defects and disorder-induced graphite (D band). Accordingly, it is confirmed that the PPy NPs were transformed to carbon nanodots through the carbonization process. Ultraviolet (UV) spectrum of the carbon nanodots exhibits a strong absorption peak at 273 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds (Fig. 4b) [36]. The UV–vis spectrum of PPy NPs exhibits both π–π* and n–π* transitions. In the UV spectrum of PPy NPs, an absorbance peak at 280 nm is shown, attributed to the π–π* interband transition in the pyrrole rings, which is a characteristic peak of PPy [37,38]. The absorption band at around 330 nm corresponds to n–π* transition because of the presence of nitrogen in pyrrole ring [39–41]. The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the carbon nanodots is compared with that of commercial blue phosphor BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+ (BAM) (Fig. 4c). In the PL spectrum of the carbon nanodots, a strong peak is observed at 448 nm when the excitation wavelength is 380 nm. The PL spectrum of the carbon nanodot is similar to that of blue phosphor. Single and strong peak indicates monodisperse size of carbon nanodots [42,43]. PL spectra are monitored in the excitation wavelength range from 380 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 4d). The emission peaks are observed in the wavelength range from 430 nm to 620 nm, exhibiting the excitation wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity. In most previous studies, the dependence of emission wavelength on excitation wavelength is one unique feature of the PL of carbon quantum dots, similar to graphene quantum dots, though the exact mechanism is still remains to be completely understood [11,15,17,44]. According to previous studies, the excitation wavelength dependent behavior is related to the surface states of carbon nanodots. In several researches, it has been found that the carbon nanodots synthesized at low temperatures have passivated-surface and exhibit excitation wavelength independent emission spectra, while the surface state of carbon nanodots prepared at higher temperatures is not passivated, hence they show excitation wavelength dependence [45,46]. Moreover, increasing the excitation wavelength (generally from 360 nm) causes the intensity of emission peak to decrease, indicating the reduction of yield [47,48]. Similar to the previous reports, the carbon nanodots obtained by carbonization of PPy NPs at high temperature represent excitation wavelength dependent emission spectra.

In summary, highly monodisperse carbon nanodots are successfully synthesized via the surfactant-free polymerization followed by carbonization process, which is a straightforward and environmental-friendly strategy, i.e. green technology. Spectroscopic properties of the carbon nanodots correspond to those of amorphous carbon nanodots, e.g. excitation dependent emission. In particular, a strong PL emission peak is observed at 448 nm as an excitation wavelength is 380 nm, which is comparable to BAM blue phosphor. This simple and green approach may open up a facile route to realize the commercialization of carbon nanodots, including the applications in photocatalyst, highly efficient fluorescent imaging, energy conversion and storage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper was supported by Konkuk University in 2018.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2019.04.059.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
